figures.caption: Figure 1. Motivation of PCA-Seg. (a) Cost aggregation comparison among different methods for class runway. (b) The prevailing serial architecture. (c) Our proposed parallel cost aggregation paradigm. The FOD strategy decouples class-level semantics and spatial context, enabling the EPL module to parse diverse knowledge from the resulting orthogonalized features.
Table 1. Comparison of performance with other state-of-the-art methods on multiple standard benchmarks in open-vocabulary semantic segmentation. See §4.2 for details.
Table 2. Comparison of zero-shot performance with other state-of-the-art methods on the Pascal-Part-116 and ADE20K-Part-234 datasets in open-vocabulary part segmentation. See §4.2 for details.

Figure 4. Comparison of qualitative results with serial cost aggregation-based state-of-the-art methods at different granularities. (a)–(d) illustrate open-vocabulary semantic segmentation on the PC-59 dataset, while (e)–(h) present open-vocabulary part segmentation on the Pascal-Part-116 benchmark. See §4.2 for details.
Figure 6. Knowledge coupling analysis between class-level semantics and spatial context under different designs. SCA/PCA denote serial/parallel cost aggregation. See §4.3 for details.

Figure 3. The framework of PCA-Seg on OVSS. Image and text features from CLIP’s visual and text encoders are combined via the Hadamard product to construct the cost volume S. This volume is then processed simultaneously through spatial and class aggregation to produce the spatial context feature Bn and the class-level semantic representation En. The multi-expert (ME-) parser extracts diverse and complementary knowledge from these two streams across multiple perspectives, while the coefficient (Co-) mapper adaptively learns weights to integrate the features parsed by the experts. Bn and En are decoupled using the feature orthogonalization decoupling (FOD) strategy to reduce redundancy, thereby providing enriched representations for expert-driven perceptual learning (EPL).
